narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 51% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. The main driver pushing the model toward this concerning prediction was your LSAT score of 23, which stands well below the average of about 37 for students who went on to pass the bar, making it the single strongest factor signaling higher risk of failure. In contrast, your lower-middle family income bracket worked in your favor, as it aligned more closely with distributions among successful bar takers than might be expected, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success. Similarly, your Year 1 grade decile of 4 provided a modest positive nudge, since it wasn't as low as the model has seen drag others down dramatically, even though it's under the average of 6 for passers. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, Year 3 decile of 3, and part-time enrollment status also factored in but had less sway overall compared to these top influences. When stacked against typical admitted students—who often have stronger early grades, higher LSATs, full-time status, and more commonly middle-to-upper family incomes—your profile showed gaps especially in standardized testing and later academic deciles that the model weighs heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven forecast based on how these elements have historically correlated with bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to 37 or above, as it's the most impactful lever here, and perhaps strengthening your recent grades through targeted coursework or tutoring. Exploring full-time options could also align better with successful patterns. Many students in similar spots have improved their profiles and succeeded—reach out to admissions for feedback on reapplying after addressing these areas, and know that persistence often pays off.
